questions.question: Which pillar of OOP is concerned with hiding the internal implementation details of a class from other objects, typically achieved using access modifiers?
options: Encapsulation
Abstraction
Polymorphism
Inheritance
questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides properties/implementation details from other objects and is achieved with access modifiers. Abstraction is about modeling only the essential features (the 'what'), which is a distinct concern that encapsulation helps enforce.

questions.question: Polymorphism in OOP primarily allows you to do which of the following?
options: Invoke a derived-class method through a base-class reference, sharing one interface across different behaviors
Create a new class by reusing the code of an existing class
Hide the data and operations of an object behind access modifiers
Model only the essential features of a thing while ignoring background details
questions.explanation: Polymorphism lets classes share the same interface while providing different functionality, invoking derived-class methods through a base-class reference. The other options describe inheritance, encapsulation, and abstraction respectively.

questions.question: In composition, what is the defining constraint on the lifetime of the contained ('part') object relative to its container ('whole')?
options: The part cannot exist without the whole; it dies with the container
The part can outlive the whole and be shared elsewhere
The part and whole always have completely independent lifetimes
The part must be created before the whole and destroyed after it
questions.explanation: Composition is a restricted aggregation in which the contained object cannot exist without the container (e.g., House owns Rooms). Aggregation, by contrast, allows the part to outlive the whole.

questions.question: The recommended solution to the diamond problem is best summarized as which principle?
options: Favor composition over inheritance ('Contain and Delegate')
Use deeper inheritance hierarchies to disambiguate the base
Always make the shared base class abstract
Override every inherited method to remove ambiguity
questions.explanation: The reviewer's solution is 'Contain and Delegate' — favor composition over inheritance — to sidestep the ambiguity multiple class inheritance creates. Deeper hierarchies or forcing abstract bases do not resolve the ambiguity of which inherited member to use.

questions.question: Programming to types (interfaces) rather than to concrete classes helps maintain flexibility.
questions.explanation: The reviewer states that 'Type is equivalent to the interface of a class' and that 'Programming to types, not classes, maintains flexibility.'

questions.question: A `Team` holds a list of `Player` objects. When a `Team` is dissolved, its players continue to exist and can join other teams. Which relationship does this model?
options: Aggregation
Composition
Plain association with no directionality
Inheritance
questions.explanation: Because the part (Player) can outlive the whole (Team), this is aggregation — a directional 'has-a' where the contained object can exist independently. Composition would require the players to be destroyed with the team.

questions.question: Which statement correctly orders the generality of the three object relationships?
options: Association is the most general; aggregation is a special form of association; composition is a special form of aggregation
Composition is the most general; aggregation is a special form of composition; association is a special form of aggregation
Aggregation is the most general; association and composition are independent special cases of it
All three are unrelated relationship kinds with no containment among them
questions.explanation: The reviewer states the containment as Association ⊃ Aggregation ⊃ Composition: aggregation is a special case of association, and composition is a restricted (special) form of aggregation.

questions.question: How do abstraction and encapsulation relate to each other ?
options: Encapsulation enables a programmer to implement the desired level of abstraction by hiding implementation details
Abstraction is achieved through access modifiers, while encapsulation models essential features
They are synonyms describing the same mechanism of inheriting from a base class
Abstraction hides data while encapsulation exposes the public interface
questions.explanation: The reviewer says encapsulation 'enables a programmer to implement the desired level of abstraction.' Access modifiers belong to encapsulation, and abstraction is about modeling essential features — so the distractor swapping those is wrong.

questions.question: Which of the following are parts of a message in an OO system? (Select all that apply.)
options: A receiver object
A method name
Optional method arguments
An optional return value
The sender's call stack depth
questions.explanation: The reviewer defines a message as having four parts: a receiver object, a method name, optional method arguments, and an optional return value. The sender's call stack depth is not part of that definition.

questions.question: Given the C# below, what does `Console.WriteLine(s.Speak())` print?
options: Woof — the overriding derived method runs through the base reference
... — the static type Animal determines which method runs
A compile error, because a base reference cannot point to a derived object
Both '...' and 'Woof', because both implementations are invoked
questions.explanation: This is polymorphism: invoking a derived-class method through a base-class reference. Because Speak is virtual and overridden, dynamic dispatch selects Dog.Speak, printing 'Woof'. The static type does not govern which override runs for virtual members.

questions.question: The reviewer ties abstraction to abstract classes by noting that an abstract class is used when you need to inherit from a class but do not need to instantiate an object of that class.
questions.explanation: The reviewer states abstraction is 'done when we only need to inherit from a class (abstract class), but do not need to instantiate the object of that class.'

questions.question: Which design goal does inheritance most directly serve ?
options: Code reuse and managing generalization/specialization by extending an existing class
Hiding the implementation details of a class behind access modifiers
Allowing one interface to be backed by many different behaviors at runtime
Reducing a complex reality to only its essential, problem-relevant features
questions.explanation: Inheritance creates a new class from an existing one for code reuse and managing generalization and specialization. Hiding details is encapsulation, one-interface-many-behaviors is polymorphism, and reducing to essentials is abstraction.

questions.question: Which statements about association are correct per the reviewer? (Select all that apply.)
options: Association defines the multiplicity between objects (e.g., one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many)
Aggregation is a special form of association
In a plain association, the related objects typically have independent lifetimes
Association requires that one object cannot exist without the other
Composition is a more general relationship than association
questions.explanation: Association defines multiplicity, aggregation is a special form of association, and associated objects have independent lifetimes. Requiring one object cannot exist without the other describes composition, and composition is more restricted (not more general) than association.

questions.question: A team needs behavior from two unrelated base classes in a language that forbids multiple class inheritance. Following the guidance, what is the soundest approach?
options: Hold instances of both as fields and delegate to them (contain and delegate)
Create an intermediate class that inherits from both to merge their behavior
Copy the needed methods from both classes into the new class to avoid any references
Make the new class inherit from one base and re-declare the other's methods as abstract
questions.explanation: The reviewer's prescribed answer to the multiple-inheritance/diamond limitation is to contain and delegate — favor composition over inheritance. Inheriting from both is exactly the forbidden multiple inheritance, and copying methods duplicates code and breaks reuse.

questions.question: The reviewer distinguishes 'type' from 'class.' Which statement best captures that distinction and its practical implication?
options: Type is equivalent to the interface (capabilities/behavior) of a class, so coding against types rather than concrete classes preserves flexibility
Type and class are interchangeable, so depending on either has the same coupling effect
Type refers to a concrete implementation, while class refers only to its public contract
Type determines memory layout, while class determines behavior, so neither affects flexibility
questions.explanation: The reviewer says type is equivalent to the interface of a class and determines capabilities/behavior, and that programming to types (not classes) maintains flexibility. The distractor that reverses type and class (type = concrete) inverts the definition.

questions.question: Why does multiple class inheritance give rise to the diamond problem?
options: A class inheriting from two classes that share a common base can inherit conflicting/ambiguous versions of the same member, leaving the resolution ambiguous
Two base classes always allocate the derived object's memory twice, doubling its size unpredictably
Virtual methods cannot be dispatched when more than one base class is present
Constructors of multiple base classes cannot run, so the object is never initialized
questions.explanation: The diamond problem is the ambiguity (the 'diamond') created when a class inherits from two classes sharing a common base, so it is unclear which inherited member applies. The other options describe failures that are not the defining cause of the diamond ambiguity.

questions.question: In the C# below, which object relationship between `House` and `Room` does the code most accurately model, and why?
options: Composition — the House creates and exclusively owns the Rooms, which are not shared and effectively cease to exist when the House does
Aggregation — the Rooms are passed in from outside and can be reused by other Houses
Plain association — House merely references externally managed Rooms with independent lifetimes
Inheritance — House specializes Room to reuse its behavior
questions.explanation: The House constructs its own Rooms in a private field and never exposes or shares them, so the parts cannot exist independently of the whole — composition. Aggregation would inject the Rooms from outside so they could outlive and be shared across Houses.

questions.question: Which of the following are among the four pillars of OOP as listed in the reviewer? (Select all that apply.)
options: Abstraction
Encapsulation
Polymorphism
Composition
Delegation
questions.explanation: The four pillars are Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism. Composition is an object relationship (and a design preference over inheritance), and delegation is a technique — neither is a pillar.
